posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
The old Mondavi reserves are usually excellent value for money, but recently there has been a run up in prices on old California wines, and the wines are fetching a multiple of $28 I paid at a Morrell auction three years ago.

The wine itself is very, very solid. Complex, aged, still rich, the fruit has a slightly pluminess, but enough bright red fruit to counteract any sense of heaviness. Also some nice tertiary flavors, mostly spices and an overlay of smoke. Finish is long, the wine is completely mature and if you can find it for less that $100 well worth buying. 93 points.

Auto correct manually corrected.

posts.name: Pat Burton
posts.cooked: Cali used to be the place for bargains on aged wines. I fear people have caught on.

posts.name: Robert Alfert

I wonder what he drinks on Tuesday?

Bern’s still has a nice cache of old Mondavi Reserves that are fairly priced and perfectly mature. I had one from the mid-80s a few months ago that was lovely. I think it was around $150.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
That is a line that ages very gracefully.

The “big juice” movement kind of left them behind in social status, but a truly wonderful wine from back in the day.

The 1974 Mondavi Reserve was a wonderful wine in its prime.

posts.name: John O Sullivan
posts.cooked: had the 2007 a few weeks ago. gave it 1.5 hours in the decanter. pretty underwhelming.

posts.name: Tom Moore
posts.cooked: I had my last 99 on Sunday and it was screaming good - just entering its prime window I’d say.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
John O':

had the 2007 a few weeks ago. gave it 1.5 hours in the decanter. pretty underwhelming.

Winery sold in 2003 (IIRC) to Constellation. I’m sure changes were made.

This thread is like salt in the wound. Over the past few days I’ve been lamenting the fact that I missed out on a 10 bottle lot of 78 Mondavi Reserve that went for under $50 per bottle at a recent auction. That wine is about on par with the excellent 74.

My wife sells Mondavi so I drink a ton of it. I would say that the 2007 is too young and I don’t really agree that the wine is dramatically different after or because of the sale. At worst it is just like almost every other Cab that has become a bit more opulent post 2000. I think the wine really needs to have a dozen years to enter its prime. It, to me, is clearly a large framed, pretty-ripe cab, but just isn’t as fruity and lush as many other early drinkers. It has a decent amount of acid and tannin and it rises up vis-à-vis peers when it’s allowed to develop some secondary flavors. It often seems a little clunky until age 10. It is also a pretty solid bet to last into the tertiary phase. That’s certainly true for the pre-97 models and, while to a lesser extent, I bet recent vintages will be excellent at age 20.

The one thing I’ve noticed is a lot of variation amongst bottles of older (pre-90) cab I’ve been buying at auction. I think since these were largely not serious collector wines at release, that there are quite a few bottles that don’t have ideal provenance.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Ian Dorin:
John O':

had the 2007 a few weeks ago. gave it 1.5 hours in the decanter. pretty underwhelming.

Winery sold in 2003 (IIRC) to Constellation. I’m sure changes were made.

Fascinating book by Julia Flynn Siler: The House of Mondavi about the internal politics and sale of the winery. I listened to it in the car, and there were plenty of times I just sat in the driveway listening to the end of the chapter. If you have any interest in the history of California wine, it is well worth buying.

posts.name: Bruce Leiser_owitz
posts.cooked:
johngonzales:

The one thing I’ve noticed is a lot of variation amongst bottles of older (pre-90) cab I’ve been buying at auction. I think since these were largely not serious collector wines at release, that there are quite a few bottles that don’t have ideal provenance.

John–Back in the 70’s and early 80’s, there weren’t nearly as many temperature and humidity-controlled cellars and wine storage facilities as there are now.

Bruce

posts.name: Tom Moore
posts.cooked:
I have a handful of the 01 reserves and the Ta Kalon bottling and they have both been drinking fantastic for the last 5 years and feel like they will hang on for another decade easy.

I believe I heard that after Const bought the winery they kept their winemaking team intact and I believe they are still together? Tim Mondavi had always been the “winemaker” but I think overlooked the whole operation.

A quality winery for sure and a California Classic.

posts.name: John O Sullivan
posts.cooked:
johngonzales:

This thread is like salt in the wound. Over the past few days I’ve been lamenting the fact that I missed out on a 10 bottle lot of 78 Mondavi Reserve that went for under $50 per bottle at a recent auction. That wine is about on par with the excellent 74.

My wife sells Mondavi so I drink a ton of it. I would say that the 2007 is too young and I don’t really agree that the wine is dramatically different after or because of the sale. At worst it is just like almost every other Cab that has become a bit more opulent post 2000. I think the wine really needs to have a dozen years to enter its prime. It, to me, is clearly a large framed, pretty-ripe cab, but just isn’t as fruity and lush as many other early drinkers. It has a decent amount of acid and tannin and it rises up vis-à-vis peers when it’s allowed to develop some secondary flavors. It often seems a little clunky until age 10. It is also a pretty solid bet to last into the tertiary phase. That’s certainly true for the pre-97 models and, while to a lesser extent, I bet recent vintages will be excellent at age 20.

The one thing I’ve noticed is a lot of variation amongst bottles of older (pre-90) cab I’ve been buying at auction. I think since these were largely not serious collector wines at release, that there are quite a few bottles that don’t have ideal provenance.

Glad you wrote this, as I wouldn’t want to scare anyone away from the 2007. I reported on how it tastes to me now. I have no skills at projecting where it is in its life cycle nor where it may go. Will wait several years before trying again.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Agreed that the 1975 is a really good wine. Had it at the winery two months ago and it showed brilliantly, so well-stored examples are well worth buying.

Winemaking is still pretty classic but the fruit is ripened longer on the vine, and aged in barrel for a shorter time.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
Tom Moore:

I have a handful of the 01 reserves and the Ta Kalon bottling and they have both been drinking fantastic for the last 5 years and feel like they will hang on for another decade easy.

I believe I heard that after Const bought the winery they kept their winemaking team intact and I believe they are still together? Tim Mondavi had always been the “winemaker” but I think overlooked the whole operation.

A quality winery for sure and a California Classic.

I think that was more of a title than anything else, it’s actually been Genevieve Janssens since 1989.

My comment was more directed at the fact that the Mondavis were quite heavy in debt at the time of the sale, so I’m sure the “bean counters” at Constellation decided economic changes had to be made.
